# Introduction

- Why Rust for scientific computing?
- How the learning modules fit together
- How the examples and terminal work fit together

::: notes
This is the opening section after the title slide. Keep it as orientation, not
as a full course abstract.
:::

## Course Arc

- Start with the project workflow
- Build the core Rust mental model
- Move from syntax to data and error handling
- Finish with scientific-computing examples

::: notes
Set expectations: these slides are a map, not the course itself. The real
teaching happens through discussion, terminal demos, and live edits in the
example projects.
:::

## Audience

- Programmers who are new to Rust
- Scientists and engineers assessing Rust
- Learners who need practical examples, not language trivia
- People who will read compiler diagnostics often

::: notes
Emphasize that prior programming experience is assumed. The course does not
try to teach programming from scratch.
:::

## Working Style

- Short concept checkpoints
- Terminal work for longer code
- Small Cargo projects for practice
- Scientific-computing relevance throughout

::: notes
Mention that many examples are intentionally small. The point is to isolate one
language or workflow idea before combining ideas in the integrated examples.
:::

## The Through-Line

- Explicit types and conversions
- Ownership before larger data structures
- Traits and iterators for reusable code
- `Result`, tests, seeds, and reproducible runs
- Integrated numerical programs at the end

::: notes
This is the main narrative thread. Rust's explicitness is not just a language
quirk; it supports maintenance, reproducibility, and correctness in technical
software.
:::

## Modules 1-4: Foundations

1. Getting Started With Rust Projects
2. Scalar Computation And Numeric Basics
3. Control Flow And Program Structure
4. Ownership, Borrowing, And Mutation

::: notes
These modules establish the basic workflow and the mental model needed before
larger examples. Ownership appears early because it affects how later code is
designed.
:::

## Modules 5-9: Building Programs

5. Data Modeling With Structs And Methods
6. Reusable Abstractions With Traits
7. Collections, Iterators, And Text Data
8. Error Handling
9. Project Organization, Libraries, And Tests

::: notes
Frame this group as the transition from isolated syntax to programs that have
shape: domain types, shared behavior, data processing, recoverable failure, and
tests.
:::

## Modules 10-13: Scientific Workflows

10. Randomness And Reproducible Runs
11. Data Parallelism With Rayon
12. Integrated Numerical Example: Julia Set
13. Integrated Numerical Example: N-Body Simulation

::: notes
These modules make the scientific-computing context explicit: reproducibility,
parallelism, structured output, diagnostics, and visualization.
:::

## Example Workflow

```{.bash}
cd source-code/hello-world
cargo run
cargo check
```

- Enter one example project
- Run the code before changing it
- Make one small change
- Use compiler output as feedback

::: notes
Switch to the terminal if useful. This slide is the pattern that will repeat
throughout the course.
:::

## Shorter Course Path

- Use Modules 1-4 for the Rust core
- Add Modules 7-8 for practical data and errors
- Use the Julia set as the main integrated example
- Leave N-body as follow-up reading or an optional lab

::: notes
For a short delivery, do not try to cover every module equally. The numerical
examples should receive more time than purely syntactic material for this
audience.
:::

## Habits To Build

- `cargo check` as the edit-check loop
- Compiler diagnostics as feedback
- Explicitness around types, errors, and configuration
- Small examples first, integrated examples later
- Reproducible commands and inputs

::: notes
These are the habits that should survive after the course, even if learners do
not remember every syntax detail.
:::

## First Hands-On Module

- Open the repository structure
- Run the smallest Rust example
- Inspect `Cargo.toml` and `src/main.rs`
- Add one dependency-backed command-line tool

::: notes
Transition directly into Module 1. Keep this as a cue slide rather than a
summary slide.
:::
